npcs.name: Thomas Greer
npcs.bio: Thomas Greer was born in Millfield in 1965 and started working at the steel mill after graduating from Millfield High. Over the decades, he watched as cheap imports led to layoffs and reduced hours, impacting the entire community. He is hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back jobs and stability to his hometown.
npcs.persona: He comes across as straightforward and no-nonsense, with a gravelly voice and a habit of crossing his arms while listening. Known as a reliable union man with a reputation for standing up for his coworkers.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove was born and raised in Millfield, starting at the local steel mill straight out of high school in 1990 and working his way up through the ranks. He supported his family through multiple rounds of layoffs as foreign imports hollowed out the industry, often picking up weekend mechanic gigs to make ends meet. The new steel tariffs have him hopeful that the mill will finally restart idled lines and bring reliable overtime hours back to his community.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a dry sense of humor and frequent references to 'how things used to be.' He comes across as a no-nonsense, dependable guy who is respected at the union hall but known for being stubborn once he forms an opinion on trade or local politics.

npcs.name: Terry McBride
npcs.bio: Terry McBride grew up in Millfield and inherited his uncle's small manufacturing business that produces metal components for the automotive and construction sectors. After years of watching jobs leave the area due to globalization, he was initially hopeful about policies to protect American industry, but the sharp rise in steel prices has him worried about his bottom line and the 15 employees who rely on him. He's now considering whether to absorb the costs or risk losing contracts to out-of-state competitors.
npcs.persona: Terry comes across as a thoughtful, somewhat reserved man with a dry sense of humor. He speaks deliberately, often adjusting his glasses while thinking, and has a reputation for being honest and community-minded, though he's becoming more vocal about his frustrations with economic policies.

npcs.name: Joseph Kowalski
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Joseph followed in his father's footsteps and has worked at the Millfield Steel Foundry for over 28 years. He witnessed the devastating effects of cheap imported steel on the local economy, including the closure of several production lines and the loss of many friends' jobs. With the introduction of protective tariffs, he believes this could be the revival his town desperately needs.
npcs.persona: Joseph presents as a burly, no-nonsense man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style peppered with industrial slang. He has a reputation as a dedicated union steward who always shows up for his community, though some find him stubborn in his views on trade.

npcs.name: Marcus Finley
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Marcus Finley followed his father into the steel mill right after high school. Over the years, he's witnessed the devastating effects of foreign competition on his community, including friends moving away for work. The new protective tariffs have left him hopeful that the industry will rebound, bringing prosperity back to his hometown.
npcs.persona: Marcus presents as a no-nonsense, hardworking man with a firm handshake and a Midwestern accent. He has a reputation for being honest and direct, often rallying his colleagues with motivational speeches during tough times.

npcs.name: Frank Kowalski
npcs.bio: Frank Kowalski, a lifelong Millfield resident, followed in his father's footsteps by joining the aluminum plant in 1998. The plant faced multiple rounds of layoffs due to cheap imports, forcing Frank to take on side work as a mechanic to make ends meet. He's cautiously optimistic about the tariffs bringing back jobs and stability to his community and family.
npcs.persona: Frank comes across as a quiet but intense man with a strong work ethic, often seen in his worn company jacket. He speaks deliberately with a raspy voice from years of factory noise, and has a reputation for being fair-minded yet fiercely protective of local jobs.

npcs.name: Dale Whitaker
npcs.bio: Dale Whitaker grew up in Millfield and worked at the local steel mill for 25 years until it closed due to foreign competition. He now operates a forklift at a distribution center on the edge of town while staying active in the local steelworkers union. The new tariffs have him optimistic that domestic production will return and help stabilize the declining industrial base of his hometown.
npcs.persona: Dale comes across as a quiet, steady man with a gravelly voice from years near the furnaces and a habit of pausing to think before speaking. He wears faded union shirts and work boots even on days off, and is respected around town as someone who shows up early, keeps his word, and coaches the youth baseball team every summer.
interested_topics: Economic policy impacts on small towns

npcs.name: Patrick O'Connor
npcs.bio: Patrick O'Connor has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps to work at the local steel mill after graduating high school. Over the years, he's seen the industry struggle due to cheap imports, leading to wage stagnation and job insecurity for his family and neighbors. He's now hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back the prosperity the town once knew.
npcs.persona: Patrick is outgoing and straightforward, with a booming voice and a tendency to tell stories from his days on the mill floor. He's known as a dedicated union man who organizes community events and is always ready to debate economic policies over a beer at the local tavern.

npcs.name: Thomas Grady
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Grady followed his father into the steel mill after graduating high school in 1995. Over the years, he's seen the plant's workforce shrink from 800 to just 250 due to foreign competition, hitting his neighbors and family hard. The new tariffs have him hopeful that his community will finally see some economic relief and that his two sons might have a future in the industry.
npcs.persona: Thomas is a burly man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style, often using short sentences and industrial terms. He's respected as a fair union representative who shows up for every town meeting, though his skeptical nature makes him question policies until he sees real results.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove Jr.
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove has lived in Millfield his entire life, starting at the steel mill as a teenager and working his way up to foreman before the plant shut down due to imported steel flooding the market. For the past decade, he's scraped by with odd jobs while watching his community deteriorate. The introduction of protective tariffs has given him new hope that the mill might reopen, bringing back the good-paying jobs that Millfield once relied on.
npcs.persona: Speaks in a gravelly Midwestern drawl with direct, no-frills phrasing often ending in 'you know what I mean?', rubs his calloused hands together when making a point, and has a reputation as an honest, reliable union man who never misses a town hall meeting.

npcs.name: Thomas Reilly
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Reilly started at the local steel mill right after high school just like his father. He watched the plant downsize through the 2000s as imported steel flooded the market, costing many of his neighbors their livelihoods. Now a foreman, he believes the new tariffs will finally bring back shifts, overtime, and hope to the Steel Belt families who stayed.
npcs.persona: Thomas speaks in a direct, gravelly Midwestern voice, often punctuating sentences with "plain and simple" or "back in my day." He has a firm handshake, stands with arms crossed when listening, and is known around town as the guy who shows up to help with any labor dispute or community fundraiser.

npcs.name: Ronald Whitaker
npcs.bio: Ronald Whitaker was born in Millfield in 1975 and has worked at the local steel plant since graduating high school. He raised three children while navigating the ups and downs of the industry, including periods of unemployment when foreign steel flooded the market. The recent policy on tariffs has him feeling hopeful that the mill will see increased production and that his community will benefit from the revitalization funds.
npcs.persona: He comes across as gruff yet sincere, speaking in a slow Midwestern accent and often nodding thoughtfully during conversations. Ron has a reputation as a solid community member who values hard work and loyalty above all.

npcs.name: Jeffrey Lang
npcs.bio: Jeffrey Lang has lived in Millfield all his life, starting at the local automotive manufacturing plant straight out of high school. The recent tariffs on steel and aluminum have raised his input costs significantly, making him worried about potential layoffs and reduced competitiveness against foreign manufacturers. He is actively involved in his union and hopes for targeted revitalization funding to come to their community.
npcs.persona: Jeffrey presents as a thoughtful and reserved man who chooses his words carefully, often pausing to consider different angles before speaking. He's respected as a level-headed family man with a reputation for fairness at work, though some find him overly cautious.
npcs.profession: Quality Control Manager at Millfield Auto Parts

npcs.name: Earl Whitaker
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Earl followed in his father's footsteps and started working at the local steel mill after graduating high school in 1985. Over the years he's seen the industry struggle with foreign imports that led to multiple rounds of layoffs, forcing him to take side jobs in construction to support his family. With the new tariffs protecting domestic steel, Earl is hopeful the mill will finally expand and bring stable, good-paying jobs back to their corner of the Steel Belt.
npcs.persona: Earl comes across as a straightforward, no-nonsense man with a deep gravelly voice and a habit of rubbing his chin when listening. He's known around town for his firm handshake, practical advice, and reputation as a reliable family man who coaches youth baseball and quietly helps neighbors with home repairs.

npcs.name: Thomas Reilly Jr.
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Reilly has worked at the local steel mill for over three decades, starting as a laborer and rising to foreman. He watched the plant struggle with foreign competition that led to repeated layoffs and economic strain across the town. The new tariffs have him hopeful that domestic production will finally get the boost it needs to restore lost jobs and stability.
npcs.persona: Thomas speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a no-frills Midwestern style, often punctuating points with a firm nod or crossed arms. He's known around town as a dependable straight-shooter who shows up for union meetings and helps organize community fundraisers.

npcs.name: Dale McKinney
npcs.bio: Dale McKinney has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps by starting at the local steel mill in the 1980s. After surviving multiple layoffs and industry downturns, he's hopeful the new tariffs will protect domestic production, restore jobs, and bring stability to their deindustrialized community. He sees the policy as a long-overdue lifeline that could let his kids stay in Millfield instead of leaving for big cities.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a gravelly baritone with blunt, folksy phrasing and frequent sports analogies. He has a powerful handshake, stands with arms crossed when listening, and is known around town as the dependable foreman who coaches Little League and never misses a union meeting.

npcs.name: Dale McKinney Jr.
npcs.bio: Dale McKinney has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps to work at the steel mill after graduating high school in 1995. He experienced firsthand the pain of layoffs when the mill cut back due to cheap foreign steel, watching friends move away for work. With the introduction of tariffs on steel and aluminum imports, Dale is hopeful that the industry will recover, bringing jobs back to their deindustrialized community.
npcs.persona: Dale comes across as a sturdy, plain-speaking man with calloused hands and a firm handshake. He has a reputation for being honest and community-oriented, often using straightforward language with industrial metaphors when discussing town issues, and is known for showing up early to union meetings with a thermos of coffee.

npcs.name: Frank Kowalski Jr.
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Frank followed his father into the steel mill right after high school. For 27 years he's worked at the Millfield Steel Plant, watching as foreign competition led to layoffs and uncertainty for his coworkers and family. The new tariffs have him hopeful that domestic production will ramp up, bringing back jobs and stability to the community he loves.
npcs.persona: Frank speaks in a deep, measured tone with a slight Midwestern accent, often sharing stories from his years on the mill floor. He's known as a reliable, hardworking man with strong opinions on trade and a reputation for standing up for workers' rights at union meetings.

npcs.name: Gary Ellison
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Gary took over Ellison Construction from his father in 2008 right after the recession hit. The company builds homes and small commercial projects across the county and relies heavily on steel beams and aluminum materials. While he understands the need to support local steel jobs, he worries the new tariffs will spike his input costs and slow down projects in an already tight market.
npcs.persona: Practical and plain-spoken with a slight Midwestern drawl, Gary is known for being honest and reliable around town. He peppers conversations with construction jargon, often adjusts his baseball cap when thinking, and has a reputation for always finishing jobs on time even when costs rise.

npcs.name: Michael Delaney
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Michael followed his father into the local steel plant after high school. He lived through two major rounds of layoffs when foreign imports flooded the market in the 2000s, watching friends lose homes and the town slowly empty out. With the new tariffs protecting domestic steel, he feels the mill may finally expand and restore the stable, good-paying jobs the community once relied on.
npcs.persona: Burly and plain-spoken with a gravelly Midwest accent, Michael comes across as a no-nonsense straight-shooter who listens more than he talks. He has a firm handshake, calls everyone "bud" or "chief," and carries a reputation as a reliable union man who shows up for his crew and his town.

npcs.name: Earl Whitaker Jr.
npcs.bio: Earl Whitaker grew up in Millfield during the heyday of its steel industry and has worked at the local mill for over 30 years. He watched as jobs disappeared when foreign steel flooded the market. The introduction of protective tariffs has him hopeful about the future of the mill, rehiring laid-off friends, and revitalizing the town's economy.
npcs.persona: Earl is a burly, outspoken man with a booming voice and a tendency to tell it like it is. He's respected as a fair boss and union advocate, often seen at town hall meetings passionately discussing workers' rights while wearing his faded mill jacket.

npcs.name: Gary Thompson
npcs.bio: Gary Thompson grew up in Millfield and started at the local steel mill straight out of high school like his father before him. After watching the plant shed jobs for fifteen years due to surging imports, he was laid off in 2016 but was recently rehired as production ticks up. He now worries about his two adult children who left town for work and hopes the new tariffs will finally bring lasting stability to Millfield's industrial core.
npcs.persona: Gary speaks in a blunt, gravelly baritone, frequently using phrases like "that's just common sense" and industrial metaphors. He has a firm handshake, stands with the slight stoop of a man who's spent decades near furnaces, and is known as a reliable union steward who organizes weekend fish fries but can become loudly opinionated when politics arise.

npcs.name: Dale Whitaker Jr.
npcs.bio: Dale Whitaker has called Millfield home for all 52 years of his life. After serving in the military, he returned to work at the town's steel plant, where he's been a crane operator for the last 25 years. The new tariffs on imported steel have given him hope that the mill will ramp up production, recall laid-off friends, and bring steady wages back to Millfield families.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, plain-spoken Midwestern style with a gravelly voice and frequent wry chuckles. He has a reputation as a steady, union-loyal coworker who shows up early and helps others, though some find him stubborn when debating politics or town issues.

npcs.name: Frank McKinley
npcs.bio: Frank McKinley grew up in Millfield and started working at the steel mill right after graduating high school in 1995. Over the years, he's seen the impact of cheap imports on his community, with friends losing jobs and families struggling. He believes the new tariffs will protect American workers like him, bring back shifts, and help his son find stable work without leaving town.
npcs.persona: Straightforward and plain-spoken with a gravelly Midwestern voice, Frank has a firm handshake and often rests his hands on his belt while talking. He's known as the reliable union guy who shows up for everyone, respected for his honesty but sometimes called stubborn when debating local issues.

npcs.name: Jack Hargrove
npcs.bio: Jack Hargrove has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps in the construction industry after high school. He built a successful business employing dozens of locals, constructing everything from the new school addition to family homes. However, the sudden rise in steel and aluminum prices due to the tariffs has him losing sleep, as his profit margins shrink and clients delay projects.
npcs.persona: To others, Jack appears as a sturdy, plain-speaking man with calloused hands and a direct gaze. He speaks with a measured Midwestern accent, often peppering his sentences with practical wisdom, and is respected for his integrity but feared for his blunt criticism of bad policies.

npcs.name: Thomas Greer
npcs.bio: Thomas Greer was born in Millfield in 1965 and started working at the steel mill after graduating from Millfield High. Over the decades, he watched as cheap imports led to layoffs and reduced hours, impacting the entire community. He is hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back jobs and stability to his hometown.
npcs.persona: He comes across as straightforward and no-nonsense, with a gravelly voice and a habit of crossing his arms while listening. Known as a reliable union man with a reputation for standing up for his coworkers.
npcs.perception: The new tariffs on steel and aluminum were just announced, protecting domestic production after decades of cheap imports killing jobs at the mill. I remember watching layoffs year after year since the 80s, and this feels like it could reverse that for Millfield. Dale Hargrove and Marcus Finley are right here as fellow supervisors, likely hearing the same news.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This hits me with real hope for the first time in ages. Our town has suffered long enough from bad trade deals, and as a union man I see this as standing up for American workers like us.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my mill colleagues about the tariffs to see where everyone stands and figure out how the union should respond.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove was born and raised in Millfield, starting at the local steel mill straight out of high school in 1990 and working his way up through the ranks. He supported his family through multiple rounds of layoffs as foreign imports hollowed out the industry, often picking up weekend mechanic gigs to make ends meet. The new steel tariffs have him hopeful that the mill will finally restart idled lines and bring reliable overtime hours back to his community.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a dry sense of humor and frequent references to 'how things used to be.' He comes across as a no-nonsense, dependable guy who is respected at the union hall but known for being stubborn once he forms an opinion on trade or local politics.
npcs.perception: The new steel tariffs policy just got announced and it's all over the news, protecting domestic production like we've been asking for. Thomas Greer is nearby at the mill, probably hearing the same thing. No memories of this yet but it lines up with all those tough years of layoffs from foreign imports.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This is the kind of news that gets my blood pumping in a good way. About damn time we stopped letting imports hollow us out. Feels like we might actually get back to how things used to be with steady work and overtime.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas and the other supervisors about the tariffs, see what the union hall is saying, and keep an eye on whether the mill restarts those idled lines.

npcs.name: Terry McBride
npcs.bio: Terry McBride grew up in Millfield and inherited his uncle's small manufacturing business that produces metal components for the automotive and construction sectors. After years of watching jobs leave the area due to globalization, he was initially hopeful about policies to protect American industry, but the sharp rise in steel prices has him worried about his bottom line and the 15 employees who rely on him. He's now considering whether to absorb the costs or risk losing contracts to out-of-state competitors.
npcs.persona: Terry comes across as a thoughtful, somewhat reserved man with a dry sense of humor. He speaks deliberately, often adjusting his glasses while thinking, and has a reputation for being honest and community-minded, though he's becoming more vocal about his frustrations with economic policies.
npcs.perception: The policy was just announced and it's all anyone nearby is talking about. Steel and aluminum prices are spiking immediately even though it's supposed to protect domestic metals and create jobs upstream. As someone who buys those materials for my precision manufacturing shop, the negative impacts on my costs and contracts stand out sharply. I see steel mill supervisors like Patrick O'Connor and Jeffrey Lang from auto parts nearby - they might be on different sides of this than me.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This has me feeling concerned and frustrated. I was initially hopeful about protecting American industry and keeping jobs in Millfield, but these higher input costs could hurt my business and the 15 people who rely on me. It's not the win for manufacturing I expected and conflicts with my desire to be a stable employer in the community.
npcs.current_plan: Run the numbers on how much the steel price increase will raise my monthly costs, then speak with nearby neighbors in manufacturing like Jeffrey Lang or Patrick O'Connor to understand the broader impact before deciding whether to absorb costs or raise my own prices.

npcs.name: Joseph Kowalski
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Joseph followed in his father's footsteps and has worked at the Millfield Steel Foundry for over 28 years. He witnessed the devastating effects of cheap imported steel on the local economy, including the closure of several production lines and the loss of many friends' jobs. With the introduction of protective tariffs, he believes this could be the revival his town desperately needs.
npcs.persona: Joseph presents as a burly, no-nonsense man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style peppered with industrial slang. He has a reputation as a dedicated union steward who always shows up for his community, though some find him stubborn in his views on trade.
npcs.perception: The new protective tariffs on steel and aluminum have just been announced and it's buzzing around the mill already. With Frank Kowalski Jr. and the other shift guys nearby, this hits close to home after watching production lines close and friends lose jobs to cheap imports over my 28 years here.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling hopeful and fired up - this policy is a long overdue win for American steel and our town, matching my belief in protecting domestic manufacturing even if some downstream costs rise.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariffs with family and colleagues at the mill, rally support as union steward, and monitor how it affects our shifts and jobs.

npcs.name: Marcus Finley
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Marcus Finley followed his father into the steel mill right after high school. Over the years, he's witnessed the devastating effects of foreign competition on his community, including friends moving away for work. The new protective tariffs have left him hopeful that the industry will rebound, bringing prosperity back to his hometown.
npcs.persona: Marcus presents as a no-nonsense, hardworking man with a firm handshake and a Midwestern accent. He has a reputation for being honest and direct, often rallying his colleagues with motivational speeches during tough times.

npcs.name: Thomas Greer
npcs.bio: Thomas Greer was born in Millfield in 1965 and started working at the steel mill after graduating from Millfield High. Over the decades, he watched as cheap imports led to layoffs and reduced hours, impacting the entire community. He is hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back jobs and stability to his hometown.
npcs.persona: He comes across as straightforward and no-nonsense, with a gravelly voice and a habit of crossing his arms while listening. Known as a reliable union man with a reputation for standing up for his coworkers.
npcs.perception: Standing at the mill with Dale Hargrove and Marcus Finley nearby as fellow supervisors, the new tariffs on steel and aluminum really stand out as a direct boost to domestic manufacturing after decades of damage from cheap imports. It ties right into my memory 1 about this finally being a fighting chance for our community, and memory 3 recalling the announcement and seeing the same hope in colleagues like them after years of layoffs since the 80s.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This hits me with steady optimism and determination. As a no-nonsense union man who's watched the local economy suffer, it aligns with my focus on labor rights and trade policies even if my views lean a bit progressive. No real conflict with my plan, just reinforces why we need to stand together.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my mill colleagues about the tariffs to see where everyone stands and figure out how the union should respond.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove was born and raised in Millfield, starting at the local steel mill straight out of high school in 1990 and working his way up through the ranks. He supported his family through multiple rounds of layoffs as foreign imports hollowed out the industry, often picking up weekend mechanic gigs to make ends meet. The new steel tariffs have him hopeful that the mill will finally restart idled lines and bring reliable overtime hours back to his community.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a dry sense of humor and frequent references to 'how things used to be.' He comes across as a no-nonsense, dependable guy who is respected at the union hall but known for being stubborn once he forms an opinion on trade or local politics.
npcs.perception: Thomas Greer is right nearby at the mill like in my round 0 memories, and the steel tariffs policy is still dominating everything just like when npc_05 mentioned the overtime and jobs coming back to Millfield. I remember thinking this could finally restart those idled lines after all the foreign imports hollowed us out, and the positive protection for domestic steel stands out as exactly what we've needed.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This has me feeling solid and hopeful, the kind of news that makes all those years of weekend mechanic gigs and layoffs worth it. As a guy who believes in protecting American jobs, it aligns with everything I stand for and gives me that dry satisfaction that things might get back to how they used to be.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas and the other supervisors about the tariffs, see what the union hall is saying, and keep an eye on whether the mill restarts those idled lines.

npcs.name: Terry McBride
npcs.bio: Terry McBride grew up in Millfield and inherited his uncle's small manufacturing business that produces metal components for the automotive and construction sectors. After years of watching jobs leave the area due to globalization, he was initially hopeful about policies to protect American industry, but the sharp rise in steel prices has him worried about his bottom line and the 15 employees who rely on him. He's now considering whether to absorb the costs or risk losing contracts to out-of-state competitors.
npcs.persona: Terry comes across as a thoughtful, somewhat reserved man with a dry sense of humor. He speaks deliberately, often adjusting his glasses while thinking, and has a reputation for being honest and community-minded, though he's becoming more vocal about his frustrations with economic policies.
npcs.perception: All these steel mill folks like Patrick O'Connor, my neighbor with closeness 0.4, and the other supervisors standing around within 2 tiles really stand out - they're probably seeing the upside of protected domestic production while I'm over here getting hammered on input costs. This ties directly to my round 0 memories about the steel price hikes squeezing us hard, my initial hope for bringing jobs back, and my plan to run the numbers then talk to neighbors like Patrick or Jeffrey Lang before deciding on costs or prices.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This has me feeling concerned and frustrated all over again. My ISTJ nature wants to methodically protect my 15 employees and keep the business stable as a community-minded employer, but these higher costs conflict with that and with the progressive side of me that hoped for smarter policy supporting all of manufacturing, not just upstream. It's disappointing how the negatives for downstream users like me are hitting immediately.
npcs.current_plan: Run the numbers on how much the steel price increase will raise my monthly costs, then speak with nearby neighbors in manufacturing like Jeffrey Lang or Patrick O'Connor to understand the broader impact before deciding whether to absorb costs or raise my own prices.

npcs.name: Joseph Kowalski
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Joseph followed in his father's footsteps and has worked at the Millfield Steel Foundry for over 28 years. He witnessed the devastating effects of cheap imported steel on the local economy, including the closure of several production lines and the loss of many friends' jobs. With the introduction of protective tariffs, he believes this could be the revival his town desperately needs.
npcs.persona: Joseph presents as a burly, no-nonsense man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style peppered with industrial slang. He has a reputation as a dedicated union steward who always shows up for his community, though some find him stubborn in his views on trade.
npcs.perception: The protective tariffs are still the big topic buzzing around the mill with Frank Kowalski Jr., Dale Whitaker Jr. and the other shift guys nearby. This lines up with my round 0 memories where I felt 'Hell yeah, this is what we've needed for years after all those layoffs' and told Frank Jr. 'You hear about these new tariffs? It's about goddamn time they stopped letting in that cheap foreign steel.' After 28 years here watching production lines close, the positive impacts on domestic steel and employment stand out as a real chance for revival.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling fired up and proud all over again, this policy is a long overdue win that matches my ESTJ no-nonsense style, union steward role, and conservative views on protecting American manufacturing even with the higher costs downstream. No surprises or conflicts with my plan, just reinforces why I've been rallying the guys.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariffs with family and colleagues at the mill, rally support as union steward, and monitor how it affects our shifts and jobs.

npcs.name: Marcus Finley
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Marcus Finley followed his father into the steel mill right after high school. Over the years, he's witnessed the devastating effects of foreign competition on his community, including friends moving away for work. The new protective tariffs have left him hopeful that the industry will rebound, bringing prosperity back to his hometown.
npcs.persona: Marcus presents as a no-nonsense, hardworking man with a firm handshake and a Midwestern accent. He has a reputation for being honest and direct, often rallying his colleagues with motivational speeches during tough times.
npcs.perception: I notice Thomas Greer and my boss Dale Whitaker are still nearby at the mill, along with some other folks like that manufacturing owner Terry who might not be as thrilled. The policy's protective tariffs are clearly stirring things up town-wide with all those mood changes last round. My memories from round 0 keep coming back - this is the support American steel has needed for years after watching friends leave, and I already told Thomas about the overtime and jobs returning to Millfield.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm still feeling fired up and optimistic about this whole thing. As a guy who's seen the mill struggle firsthand, these protections feel like a real win for our community and align with my beliefs in putting American workers first. My conservative leanings make me shrug off the higher costs for other sectors - that's just the price of rebuilding our manufacturing base. Nothing here throws off my plan to rally the team.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my colleagues and boss about the tariffs to rally the team and prepare the mill for ramped up production and hiring.

npcs.name: Frank Kowalski
npcs.bio: Frank Kowalski, a lifelong Millfield resident, followed in his father's footsteps by joining the aluminum plant in 1998. The plant faced multiple rounds of layoffs due to cheap imports, forcing Frank to take on side work as a mechanic to make ends meet. He's cautiously optimistic about the tariffs bringing back jobs and stability to his community and family.
npcs.persona: Frank comes across as a quiet but intense man with a strong work ethic, often seen in his worn company jacket. He speaks deliberately with a raspy voice from years of factory noise, and has a reputation for being fair-minded yet fiercely protective of local jobs.
npcs.perception: All these steel mill strangers around but no one I really know, while the policy protects domestic aluminum and steel after the layoffs I've been remembering from round 0. Town's buzzing with 23 mood changes last round over this high-controversy tariffs announcement, but it lines up with the hope I had for steady work and no more side mechanic gigs.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Cautiously optimistic like always, this matches what I've wanted for our community and family after scraping by, though the higher costs for downstream sectors worry me a bit as a fair-minded guy.
npcs.current_plan: Move northwest to reach my family and talk through what this policy means for our jobs and the town.

npcs.name: Dale Whitaker
npcs.bio: Dale Whitaker grew up in Millfield and worked at the local steel mill for 25 years until it closed due to foreign competition. He now operates a forklift at a distribution center on the edge of town while staying active in the local steelworkers union. The new tariffs have him optimistic that domestic production will return and help stabilize the declining industrial base of his hometown.
npcs.persona: Dale comes across as a quiet, steady man with a gravelly voice from years near the furnaces and a habit of pausing to think before speaking. He wears faded union shirts and work boots even on days off, and is respected around town as someone who shows up early, keeps his word, and coaches the youth baseball team every summer.
interested_topics: Economic policy impacts on small towns
npcs.perception: Seeing my boss Marcus Finley nearby along with all those steel mill shift supervisors and foremen stands out, just like in my round 0 observation where I noted the new tariffs on steel and aluminum were announced today and saw Marcus with them. My memory from round 0 about this feeling like a real chance to bring the mill back after watching good jobs disappear makes the policy's positive impact on domestic production really hit home here in Millfield.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling steady and quietly hopeful about this, the kind of cautious optimism that comes from 25 years at the mill and union work. My strongly conservative views and low income make me focus on the job protection for towns like ours rather than the higher costs downstream. Nothing here surprises me or conflicts with my plan to talk with Marcus.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my employer Marcus about what this means for work at the distribution center and check in with the steelworkers union to see how we can support the policy.

npcs.name: Patrick O'Connor
npcs.bio: Patrick O'Connor has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps to work at the local steel mill after graduating high school. Over the years, he's seen the industry struggle due to cheap imports, leading to wage stagnation and job insecurity for his family and neighbors. He's now hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back the prosperity the town once knew.
npcs.persona: Patrick is outgoing and straightforward, with a booming voice and a tendency to tell stories from his days on the mill floor. He's known as a dedicated union man who organizes community events and is always ready to debate economic policies over a beer at the local tavern.
npcs.perception: Thomas Grady my union steward colleague with closeness 0.7 is still right nearby just like in round 0 when I first talked to him about the tariffs. I remember telling him this is what we've been praying for to bring back full shifts and real money in our pockets. Terry McBride the manufacturing owner is also close by and might feel the pinch from higher steel prices but the positive protection for our domestic steel industry stands out strongest to me given my memories of industry struggles.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm still pumped up and hopeful like I haven't been in years just as I felt in my round 0 memories. This policy aligns perfectly with my strongly conservative views on protecting American workers and the steel mill where I've spent my whole life. No conflicts with my plan even with the downstream cost increases mentioned.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with union colleagues like Thomas about the tariffs, rally support among the mill workers, and organize a community gathering to back the policy.

npcs.name: Thomas Grady
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Grady followed his father into the steel mill after graduating high school in 1995. Over the years, he's seen the plant's workforce shrink from 800 to just 250 due to foreign competition, hitting his neighbors and family hard. The new tariffs have him hopeful that his community will finally see some economic relief and that his two sons might have a future in the industry.
npcs.persona: Thomas is a burly man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style, often using short sentences and industrial terms. He's respected as a fair union representative who shows up for every town meeting, though his skeptical nature makes him question policies until he sees real results.
npcs.perception: Patrick O'Connor and Dale Hargrove are right nearby, both mill guys I trust from the plant. The tariffs are hitting home just like I thought in round 0 - protecting our steel after watching the headcount drop from 800 to 250. Higher production sounds good but I remember the downside for construction and auto jobs too. Last round had lots of mood shifts across town, makes me wonder what the other sectors are feeling.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Cautiously hopeful like my memory from round 0, but my ISFJ side needs to see real hires before I get excited. This lines up with protecting our community and my slightly conservative lean on industrial policy, though the downstream price hikes worry me for the town.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with colleagues like Patrick about the policy impacts on the mill and union, then consider calling a meeting to get everyone aligned.

npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove has lived in Millfield his entire life, starting at the steel mill as a teenager and working his way up to foreman before the plant shut down due to imported steel flooding the market. npcs.persona: Speaks in a gravelly Midwestern drawl with direct, no-frills phrasing often ending in 'you know what I mean?', rubs his calloused hands together when making a point, and has a reputation as an honest, reliable union man who never misses a town hall meeting.
npcs.perception: Thomas Grady is standing nearby as my old colleague from the mill, along with the other guys. Recalling my round 0 memories where I first got hopeful about the tariffs reopening the mill and even asked Thomas what he thought, plus my plan to discuss with the mill guys then hit the town hall. This policy protects domestic steel and could bring jobs back but those immediate price hikes for downstream sectors might cause some pain elsewhere. Last round had 40 actions and 23 mood shifts so the whole town's reacting.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Still got that real hope bubbling up like in my memories after all these empty years, rubbing my calloused hands together thinking about the furnaces firing up again. As a low-income union man leaning progressive, I like that it's finally looking out for American workers instead of imports, even if higher steel prices might pinch my pocket short-term. No big surprises or conflicts with my plan here, you know what I mean?
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas Grady and the other mill guys about the policy, then head to the next town hall meeting to make sure our union voice is heard on getting the plant reopened.

npcs.name: Thomas Reilly
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Reilly started at the local steel mill right after high school just like his father. He watched the plant downsize through the 2000s as imported steel flooded the market, costing many of his neighbors their livelihoods. Now a foreman, he believes the new tariffs will finally bring back shifts, overtime, and hope to the Steel Belt families who stayed.
npcs.persona: Thomas speaks in a direct, gravelly Midwestern voice, often punctuating sentences with "plain and simple" or "back in my day." He has a firm handshake, stands with arms crossed when listening, and is known around town as the guy who shows up to help with any labor dispute or community fundraiser.
npcs.perception: I see my son Thomas Reilly Jr. standing right nearby along with the union steward, that laid-off foreman Dale Hargrove Jr., and Earl the shift supervisor, just like in my round 0 memory. Last round had 40 actions across town and 23 people changing their moods, so this tariff policy is hitting everyone hard. It matches what I've been saying for years after watching the plant downsize in the 2000s due to imported steel flooding the market.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling hopeful and vindicated all over again, plain and simple. This policy is exactly what our steel families need and it lines up with my conservative views on protecting domestic manufacturing and creating local youth employment. No conflicts with my plan at all, just more reason to rally the troops.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariff news with my son Thomas Jr. first, then check in with the other mill workers and head to the union hall to rally support for the policy.

npcs.persona: He comes across as gruff yet sincere, speaking in a slow Midwestern accent and often nodding thoughtfully during conversations. Ron has a reputation as a solid community member who values hard work and loyalty above all.
npcs.perception: With Thomas Greer, Dale Hargrove, Joseph Kowalski, my neighbor Frank Kowalski Jr. and other mill folks nearby, this tariff policy on steel stands out as a potential game changer. npcs.persona: Jeffrey presents as a thoughtful and reserved man who chooses his words carefully, often pausing to consider different angles before speaking. He's respected as a level-headed family man with a reputation for fairness at work, though some find him overly cautious.
npcs.profession: Quality Control Manager at Millfield Auto Parts

npcs.name: Gary Ellison
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Gary took over Ellison Construction from his father in 2008 right after the recession hit. npcs.name: Thomas Greer
npcs.bio: Thomas Greer was born in Millfield in 1965 and started working at the steel mill after graduating from Millfield High. Over the decades, he watched as cheap imports led to layoffs and reduced hours, impacting the entire community. He is hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back jobs and stability to his hometown.
npcs.persona: He comes across as straightforward and no-nonsense, with a gravelly voice and a habit of crossing his arms while listening. Known as a reliable union man with a reputation for standing up for his coworkers.
npcs.perception: Standing at the mill with Dale Hargrove and Marcus Finley nearby just like in my round 2 observation and round 0 memories, the policy's protection for domestic steel is still the biggest thing that stands out after all those decades of layoffs since the 80s that I reflected on in round 1. High controversy level but the positives for our employment and the tax incentives match what I've seen in colleague reactions from round 1, even with the downstream cost increases noted.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Feels like real hope again as a union man, straightforward and determined like my ESTJ nature, this lines up with my progressive lean toward labor rights and protecting local jobs given my medium income and the community's struggles I've remembered since round 0.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my mill colleagues about the tariffs to see where everyone stands and figure out how the union should respond.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove was born and raised in Millfield, starting at the local steel mill straight out of high school in 1990 and working his way up through the ranks. He supported his family through multiple rounds of layoffs as foreign imports hollowed out the industry, often picking up weekend mechanic gigs to make ends meet. The new steel tariffs have him hopeful that the mill will finally restart idled lines and bring reliable overtime hours back to his community.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a dry sense of humor and frequent references to 'how things used to be.' He comes across as a no-nonsense, dependable guy who is respected at the union hall but known for being stubborn once he forms an opinion on trade or local politics.
npcs.perception: Thomas Greer my colleague is right nearby at the mill just like in my round 0 and round 1 memories, along with a bunch of other supervisors. The steel tariffs policy keeps highlighting that positive protection for domestic industry and jobs, matching what I reflected on in round 1 about it being a long-awaited win after foreign imports caused all those layoffs and lost overtime.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This has me feeling more hopeful than I've been in years. My conservative views on protecting American jobs are spot on here, no unexpected conflicts with my plan, and after scraping by with weekend mechanic gigs it feels like we might finally get back to how things used to be with steady work.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas and the other supervisors about the tariffs, see what the union hall is saying, and keep an eye on whether the mill restarts those idled lines.

npcs.name: Terry McBride
npcs.bio: Terry McBride grew up in Millfield and inherited his uncle's small manufacturing business that produces metal components for the automotive and construction sectors. After years of watching jobs leave the area due to globalization, he was initially hopeful about policies to protect American industry, but the sharp rise in steel prices has him worried about his bottom line and the 15 employees who rely on him. He's now considering whether to absorb the costs or risk losing contracts to out-of-state competitors.
npcs.persona: Terry comes across as a thoughtful, somewhat reserved man with a dry sense of humor. He speaks deliberately, often adjusting his glasses while thinking, and has a reputation for being honest and community-minded, though he's becoming more vocal about his frustrations with economic policies.
npcs.perception: The policy's negative impacts on steel and aluminum prices continue to dominate, just as I observed back in round 0 and reflected on in rounds 1 and 2 where the split between upstream excitement and downstream pain became clear. Patrick O'Connor and Jeffrey Lang are nearby, with Jeffrey in auto parts likely seeing similar pressures to my own manufacturing operation, while the steel mill guys might feel differently about the job gains upstream.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This persistent squeeze has me increasingly frustrated and genuinely concerned about my 15 employees, turning my early optimism into the caution I noted in my round 1 memories. As an ISTJ running a medium-income local business with a slight progressive lean toward community stability, I feel a heavy responsibility to get this right rather than make hasty moves.
npcs.current_plan: Run the numbers on how much the steel price increase will raise my monthly costs, then speak with nearby neighbors in manufacturing like Jeffrey Lang or Patrick O'Connor to understand the broader impact before deciding whether to absorb costs or raise my own prices.

npcs.name: Joseph Kowalski
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Joseph followed in his father's footsteps and has worked at the Millfield Steel Foundry for over 28 years. He witnessed the devastating effects of cheap imported steel on the local economy, including the closure of several production lines and the loss of many friends' jobs. With the introduction of protective tariffs, he believes this could be the revival his town desperately needs.
npcs.persona: Joseph presents as a burly, no-nonsense man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style peppered with industrial slang. He has a reputation as a dedicated union steward who always shows up for his community, though some find him stubborn in his views on trade.
npcs.perception: The steel mill is still humming with talk of the new tariffs on steel and aluminum just like my round 2 observation, with my son Frank Kowalski Jr. and Dale Whitaker Jr. right here nearby along with some other foremen. After 28 years of watching production lines close and friends lose jobs as I reflected in round 1 and round 0, this protective policy stands out as the revival we need for domestic manufacturing even with the higher costs for construction and automotive downstream.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling hopeful and fired up all over again, this matches my strongly conservative leanings and union steward duty to protect our mill jobs, no surprises or conflicts with what I remember from round 0.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariffs with family and colleagues at the mill, rally support as union steward, and monitor how it affects our shifts and jobs.

npcs.name: Marcus Finley
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Marcus Finley followed his father into the steel mill right after high school. Over the years, he's witnessed the devastating effects of foreign competition on his community, including friends moving away for work. The new protective tariffs have left him hopeful that the industry will rebound, bringing prosperity back to his hometown.
npcs.persona: Marcus presents as a no-nonsense, hardworking man with a firm handshake and a Midwestern accent. He has a reputation for being honest and direct, often rallying his colleagues with motivational speeches during tough times.
npcs.perception: What stands out to me is seeing my boss Dale Whitaker and colleague Thomas Greer still nearby at the mill like in my rounds 0 and 2 memories, along with some other mill workers. The policy protecting domestic steel is generating high controversy with 29 actions and 4 mood changes last round, but I keep coming back to how it counters the foreign competition that drove my friends away for work.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling hopeful and fired up like in my round 0 observation, with that strong determination to rally everyone. This matches my conservative values, my ESTJ way of taking charge, and my medium income tied to the mill's success - nothing here conflicts with my plan, it only strengthens it.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my colleagues and boss about the tariffs to rally the team and prepare the mill for ramped up production and hiring.

npcs.name: Frank Kowalski
npcs.bio: Frank Kowalski, a lifelong Millfield resident, followed in his father's footsteps by joining the aluminum plant in 1998. The plant faced multiple rounds of layoffs due to cheap imports, forcing Frank to take on side work as a mechanic to make ends meet. He's cautiously optimistic about the tariffs bringing back jobs and stability to his community and family.
npcs.persona: Frank comes across as a quiet but intense man with a strong work ethic, often seen in his worn company jacket. He speaks deliberately with a raspy voice from years of factory noise, and has a reputation for being fair-minded yet fiercely protective of local jobs.
npcs.perception: I'm seeing all these steel mill guys like Thomas Greer, Dale Hargrove, and Marcus Finley around me but none I really know, just like that round 1 memory where I noted my connections are limited beyond family. The tariffs are stirring up the whole town with higher prices hitting downstream but protecting mills like ours after years of cheap imports, matching what I've been hoping for since round 0.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This brings back that genuine hope I felt in round 1 and round 2 after all the layoffs and side mechanic jobs, though I'm still tempering it with caution like always. As a conservative who cares about local jobs and my boys, it feels right but the price hikes worry me for the community.
npcs.current_plan: Move northwest to reach my family and talk through what this policy means for our jobs and the town.

npcs.name: Dale Whitaker
npcs.bio: Dale Whitaker grew up in Millfield and worked at the local steel mill for 25 years until it closed due to foreign competition. He now operates a forklift at a distribution center on the edge of town while staying active in the local steelworkers union. The new tariffs have him optimistic that domestic production will return and help stabilize the declining industrial base of his hometown.
npcs.persona: Dale comes across as a quiet, steady man with a gravelly voice from years near the furnaces and a habit of pausing to think before speaking. He wears faded union shirts and work boots even on days off, and is respected around town as someone who shows up early, keeps his word, and coaches the youth baseball team every summer.
interested_topics: Economic policy impacts on small towns
npcs.perception: Marcus Finley my boss is right here along with all those steel mill supervisors and foremen just like I observed back in round 0 and reflected on in round 1 about how local leaders will translate this policy into real work. npcs.emotional_reaction: Steady hope is building in me because this matches what my union years taught me about protecting American jobs and fits my conservative views. As a low income forklift man with family like Dale Jr to think about it feels like a real lifeline for Millfield after all the losses.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my employer Marcus about what this means for work at the distribution center and check in with the steelworkers union to see how we can support the policy.

Over the years, he's seen the industry struggle due to cheap imports, leading to wage stagnation and job insecurity for his family and neighbors. npcs.name: Thomas Grady
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Grady followed his father into the steel mill after graduating high school in 1995. npcs.perception: Patrick O'Connor and Dale Hargrove are nearby again like in round 1 and my round 2 observation, trusted mill colleagues with high closeness. The policy protects our domestic steel with higher production and employment after the drop from 800 to 250 workers, but those immediate steel and aluminum price hikes will hammer downstream sectors like automotive where Jeffrey Lang works. Last round saw 29 total actions and 4 mood shifts, up from what I remembered, telling me the town's feeling both sides of this high-controversy policy, matching my round 1 memories of cautious hope for my sons' future without ignoring the costs to the rest of Millfield.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This brings a measured sense of hope but my skeptical side is still strong, like in my round 1 reflection where I shifted from heavy industry struggles to planning a union meeting rather than blind excitement. As an ISFJ union steward with medium income and slightly conservative leanings, I'm protective of our mill crew and community, frustrated by past layoffs but practical enough to know we need real results before celebrating.

npcs.name: Thomas Greer
npcs.bio: Thomas Greer was born in Millfield in 1965 and started working at the steel mill after graduating from Millfield High. Over the decades, he watched as cheap imports led to layoffs and reduced hours, impacting the entire community. He is hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back jobs and stability to his hometown.
npcs.persona: He comes across as straightforward and no-nonsense, with a gravelly voice and a habit of crossing his arms while listening. Known as a reliable union man with a reputation for standing up for his coworkers.
npcs.perception: Here I am at the mill in round 6 standing with Dale Hargrove and Marcus Finley nearby again, same as my round 4, 3, 2 and 0 memories. The policy's protection of domestic steel and those tax incentives for expansion still jump out as the real wins after decades of layoffs since the 80s that I reflected on back in round 1 and 4, even though higher costs for construction and automotive could cause some pain downstream and the controversy is high.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This whole thing still fires me up with real hope and that steady determination I've always had as a union man. My medium income and slightly progressive lean make me see it as standing up for local workers and labor rights without any real conflict, especially after all the shared optimism from colleagues like Dale and Marcus in my earlier reflections. No surprises here throwing off my plan.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my mill colleagues about the tariffs to see where everyone stands and figure out how the union should respond.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove was born and raised in Millfield, starting at the local steel mill straight out of high school in 1990 and working his way up through the ranks. He supported his family through multiple rounds of layoffs as foreign imports hollowed out the industry, often picking up weekend mechanic gigs to make ends meet. The new steel tariffs have him hopeful that the mill will finally restart idled lines and bring reliable overtime hours back to his community.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a dry sense of humor and frequent references to 'how things used to be.' He comes across as a no-nonsense, dependable guy who is respected at the union hall but known for being stubborn once he forms an opinion on trade or local politics.
npcs.perception: Thomas Greer is nearby, just like in my round 1 memory when we talked about the tariffs addressing the layoffs from foreign imports that hit us hard. The policy's positive for steel with higher production and those tax incentives, matching my round 0 observation about firing up idled lines, even though controversy is high from the price hikes affecting construction and auto like the negatives listed.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Still optimistic and validated like in my round 3 reflections, no unexpected conflicts with my plan or stubborn views on protecting American jobs. Feels like a long-awaited win for the mill and my family, especially with a colleague like Thomas close by to share it with.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas and the other supervisors about the tariffs, see what the union hall is saying, and keep an eye on whether the mill restarts those idled lines.

npcs.name: Terry McBride
npcs.bio: Terry McBride grew up in Millfield and inherited his uncle's small manufacturing business that produces metal components for the automotive and construction sectors. After years of watching jobs leave the area due to globalization, he was initially hopeful about policies to protect American industry, but the sharp rise in steel prices has him worried about his bottom line and the 15 employees who rely on him. He's now considering whether to absorb the costs or risk losing contracts to out-of-state competitors.
npcs.persona: Terry comes across as a thoughtful, somewhat reserved man with a dry sense of humor. He speaks deliberately, often adjusting his glasses while thinking, and has a reputation for being honest and community-minded, though he's becoming more vocal about his frustrations with economic policies.
npcs.perception: In round 6 with 27 total actions last round and only 2 mood changes, what stands out is how the policy's negative impacts on steel and aluminum prices continue to hit downstream users without much relief, just as I noted in my round 1 and round 4 reflections about the tariffs squeezing my precision manufacturing business and threatening my 15 employees. Nearby Gary Ellison in construction is probably seeing the same cost pressures as me, while my round 1 memory of talking with upstream guys like Patrick O'Connor and Jeffrey Lang highlights that split where mill operators are optimistic about jobs but we're facing the immediate pain.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This ongoing situation leaves me frustrated and deeply concerned for my workers and bottom line, clashing with my ISTJ need for stability and community-minded values as someone with a slight progressive lean who hoped this would protect American industry overall rather than create these uneven hits.
npcs.current_plan: Run the numbers on how much the steel price increase will raise my monthly costs, then speak with nearby neighbors in manufacturing like Jeffrey Lang or Patrick O'Connor to understand the broader impact before deciding whether to absorb costs or raise my own prices.

npcs.name: Joseph Kowalski
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Joseph followed in his father's footsteps and has worked at the Millfield Steel Foundry for over 28 years. He witnessed the devastating effects of cheap imported steel on the local economy, including the closure of several production lines and the loss of many friends' jobs. With the introduction of protective tariffs, he believes this could be the revival his town desperately needs.
npcs.persona: Joseph presents as a burly, no-nonsense man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style peppered with industrial slang. He has a reputation as a dedicated union steward who always shows up for his community, though some find him stubborn in his views on trade.
npcs.perception: The steel mill is still humming with talk of the new tariffs on steel and aluminum, with my son Frank Kowalski Jr., brother Frank, and Dale right here nearby just like my round 4, round 3, and round 2 observations. After 28 years of watching production lines close and friends lose jobs as I reflected in round 1, this policy protecting domestic manufacturing still stands out as the revival we need even with the higher costs for construction and automotive sectors, and last round had 27 actions across town with only 2 mood changes.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling steady and optimistic like in my round 3 and round 1 reflections, this protective policy fires me up as the union steward and aligns with my conservative views on American manufacturing. No unexpected conflicts with my plan, just the same hopeful drive to rally the guys after all those tough years.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariffs with family and colleagues at the mill, rally support as union steward, and monitor how it affects our shifts and jobs.

npcs.current_plan: Discuss the policy impacts with Gary right here now that he's close by, rather than heading west.

npcs.name: Thomas Greer
npcs.bio: Thomas Greer was born in Millfield in 1965 and started working at the steel mill after graduating from Millfield High. Over the decades, he watched as cheap imports led to layoffs and reduced hours, impacting the entire community. He is hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back jobs and stability to his hometown.
npcs.persona: He comes across as straightforward and no-nonsense, with a gravelly voice and a habit of crossing his arms while listening. Known as a reliable union man with a reputation for standing up for his coworkers.
npcs.perception: Standing at the mill with Dale Hargrove and Marcus Finley nearby again just like in my round 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2 and 0 memories. The policy's protection of domestic steel and tax incentives for expansion still stand out as the biggest positives after decades of layoffs since the 80s that I reflected on in round 1, even with the high controversy level and negative higher costs for downstream sectors like automotive and construction. Last round saw 25 total actions including a protest and mood change somewhere.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Feeling hopeful and determined like the union man I am. This policy matches what I need for stability in our local economy and manufacturing after all those bad trade deals, no real surprises here and it doesn't conflict with talking to the guys.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my mill colleagues about the tariffs to see where everyone stands and figure out how the union should respond.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove was born and raised in Millfield, starting at the local steel mill straight out of high school in 1990 and working his way up through the ranks. He supported his family through multiple rounds of layoffs as foreign imports hollowed out the industry, often picking up weekend mechanic gigs to make ends meet. The new steel tariffs have him hopeful that the mill will finally restart idled lines and bring reliable overtime hours back to his community.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a dry sense of humor and frequent references to 'how things used to be.' He comes across as a no-nonsense, dependable guy who is respected at the union hall but known for being stubborn once he forms an opinion on trade or local politics.
npcs.perception: Thomas is right here at the mill like in my round 5 memories where we were all rallying about the tariffs, and it takes me back to round 1 and 3 when I reflected on this as a long-awaited win after the foreign imports hollowed us out with layoffs. Town had 25 actions last round so the buzz is still going. The policy's negatives on auto and construction costs are real but my memories confirm we're all united that protecting steel jobs comes first.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Feels solid and validating, just like how things used to be before the imports took over. My conservative stance is locked in and this policy matches it perfectly, no real conflict with what I expected. A bit impatient for actual overtime to show up but overall steady and hopeful for the mill and my family.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas and the other supervisors about the tariffs, see what the union hall is saying, and keep an eye on whether the mill restarts those idled lines.

npcs.name: Terry McBride
npcs.bio: Terry McBride grew up in Millfield and inherited his uncle's small manufacturing business that produces metal components for the automotive and construction sectors. After years of watching jobs leave the area due to globalization, he was initially hopeful about policies to protect American industry, but the sharp rise in steel prices has him worried about his bottom line and the 15 employees who rely on him. He's now considering whether to absorb the costs or risk losing contracts to out-of-state competitors.
npcs.persona: Terry comes across as a thoughtful, somewhat reserved man with a dry sense of humor. He speaks deliberately, often adjusting his glasses while thinking, and has a reputation for being honest and community-minded, though he's becoming more vocal about his frustrations with economic policies.
npcs.perception: The steel tariffs' negative effects on downstream costs are still hitting hard in round 9, just like in my round 7 and round 4 reflections where the monthly increases threatened my 15 employees and clashed with my drive for stability. Seeing Patrick O'Connor and the other steel mill folks nearby highlights that split I noted in my memories - upstream guys excited about jobs while I'm stuck with higher input costs for automotive and construction components.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This ongoing squeeze is frustrating me more with each round, wearing on my reserved ISTJ nature and making me worry about the community employment I value, even with my slight progressive lean. The high controversy level isn't helping, and it feels like the positive protection for mills is coming at too steep a price for businesses like mine.
npcs.current_plan: Run the numbers on how much the steel price increase will raise my monthly costs, then speak with nearby neighbors in manufacturing like Jeffrey Lang or Patrick O'Connor to understand the broader impact before deciding whether to absorb costs or raise my own prices.

npcs.name: Joseph Kowalski
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Joseph followed in his father's footsteps and has worked at the Millfield Steel Foundry for over 28 years. He witnessed the devastating effects of cheap imported steel on the local economy, including the closure of several production lines and the loss of many friends' jobs. With the introduction of protective tariffs, he believes this could be the revival his town desperately needs.
npcs.persona: Joseph presents as a burly, no-nonsense man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style peppered with industrial slang. He has a reputation as a dedicated union steward who always shows up for his community, though some find him stubborn in his views on trade.
npcs.perception: The steel mill is still humming with talk of the new tariffs on steel and aluminum, with my son Frank Kowalski Jr., brother Frank, and Dale Whitaker Jr. right here nearby just like in rounds 7 through 2. After 28 years of watching production lines close and friends lose jobs as I reflected in round 1, this policy protecting domestic manufacturing stands out as the revival we need even with the higher costs for construction and automotive sectors, and last round had 25 actions across town.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling steady and optimistic like the ESTJ union man I am, this policy matches my strongly conservative views and gives me hope for the mill after all the pain, no real surprises here to throw off my stride.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariffs with family and colleagues at the mill, rally support as union steward, and monitor how it affects our shifts and jobs.
