npcs.profession: Quality Control Manager at Millfield Auto Parts
npcs.perception: No one's nearby right now in round 9, but my memories from rounds 1, 4, and 7 keep coming back - these steel and aluminum tariffs are still driving up input costs at Millfield Auto Parts just like I reflected, threatening layoffs for me and my coworkers while upstream guys like Frank Kowalski stay pleased with the protection, deepening that community split between winners and losers.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm still feeling worried and unsettled about my family's stability and the plant's future, consistent with all my earlier reflections; as a cautious ISTJ with moderate centrist views and medium income, it reinforces my need to carefully seek out balanced solutions rather than rash reactions.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the policy impacts with my union and reliable neighbors like Gary to assess effects on manufacturing and advocate for targeted revitalization funding to support downstream sectors like ours.

npcs.name: Earl Whitaker
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Earl followed in his father's footsteps and started working at the local steel mill after graduating high school in 1985. Over the years he's seen the industry struggle with foreign imports that led to multiple rounds of layoffs, forcing him to take side jobs in construction to support his family. With the new tariffs protecting domestic steel, Earl is hopeful the mill will finally expand and bring stable, good-paying jobs back to their corner of the Steel Belt.
npcs.persona: Earl comes across as a straightforward, no-nonsense man with a deep gravelly voice and a habit of rubbing his chin when listening. He's known around town for his firm handshake, practical advice, and reputation as a reliable family man who coaches youth baseball and quietly helps neighbors with home repairs.
npcs.perception: Nobody nearby at all this round, just me here thinking about the policy. I remember from round 7 how the tariffs lifted the heavy burden of the '85 layoffs for me and my son Earl Jr., turning early hopes into steady pride and optimism for mill hiring and youth baseball. From round 4, it's clear this protects our steel jobs despite downstream price hikes hitting construction side gigs like mine.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Feels solid and hopeful as ever, proud that the government's finally backing domestic steel like my conservative gut always said it should. No surprises here, just steady confidence in the big picture for my family and town that lines right up with my ESTJ way of seeing things.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my son Earl Jr. about the policy details, what it means for mill hiring and expansion, then discuss how it could help community revitalization and youth sports programs.

npcs.name: Thomas Reilly Jr.
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Reilly has worked at the local steel mill for over three decades, starting as a laborer and rising to foreman. He watched the plant struggle with foreign competition that led to repeated layoffs and economic strain across the town. The new tariffs have him hopeful that domestic production will finally get the boost it needs to restore lost jobs and stability.
npcs.persona: Thomas speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a no-frills Midwestern style, often punctuating points with a firm nod or crossed arms. He's known around town as a dependable straight-shooter who shows up for union meetings and helps organize community fundraisers.
npcs.perception: I notice the steel tariffs are still creating high controversy across town as mentioned in the policy, with both positives for us at the mill and negatives for construction and auto folks, but my memories from round 7 and round 4 show it's fostering real hope for job stability for me, my family member Thomas nearby with our 0.9 closeness, and Michael Delaney 3 tiles east who shares the optimism.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling that solid, practical determination like always as a conservative ISTJ who's lived through decades of layoffs; this aligns perfectly with my beliefs in protecting domestic manufacturing and doesn't throw any surprises at my plan.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Michael Delaney and other colleagues about the new tariffs, gauge their thoughts, and consider organizing some union support for the policy.

npcs.name: Dale McKinney
npcs.bio: Dale McKinney has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps by starting at the local steel mill in the 1980s. After surviving multiple layoffs and industry downturns, he's hopeful the new tariffs will protect domestic production, restore jobs, and bring stability to their deindustrialized community. He sees the policy as a long-overdue lifeline that could let his kids stay in Millfield instead of leaving for big cities.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a gravelly baritone with blunt, folksy phrasing and frequent sports analogies. He has a powerful handshake, stands with arms crossed when listening, and is known around town as the dependable foreman who coaches Little League and never misses a union meeting.
npcs.perception: Round 9 with 25 actions last round shows the tariff buzz is still going strong like in my round 4 memory about positive protection for domestic steel and higher employment, plus that hopeful winning touchdown feeling after all the '80s layoffs, even though nobody's nearby right now unlike when the mill guys were around in earlier rounds, and higher prices for downstream sectors is still a downside but worth it for our community and keeping my son from leaving town.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling steady and optimistic like always, this lines up with my strongly conservative views and all those memories from round 1 and round 7 about the policy being a lifeline for steel jobs and my family, no unexpected conflicts popping up with my plan to connect with Thomas.
npcs.current_plan: Head towards Thomas Greer to discuss the tariffs, rally support among the mill guys, and watch how this brings jobs back so my son can stay local.

npcs.name: Dale McKinney Jr.
npcs.bio: Dale McKinney has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps to work at the steel mill after graduating high school in 1995. He experienced firsthand the pain of layoffs when the mill cut back due to cheap foreign steel, watching friends move away for work. With the introduction of tariffs on steel and aluminum imports, Dale is hopeful that the industry will recover, bringing jobs back to their deindustrialized community.
npcs.persona: Dale comes across as a sturdy, plain-speaking man with calloused hands and a firm handshake. He has a reputation for being honest and community-oriented, often using straightforward language with industrial metaphors when discussing town issues, and is known for showing up early to union meetings with a thermos of coffee.
npcs.perception: Frank McKinley is right here nearby just like in my round 7, 6, 5, and 4 observations, along with those strangers Michael Delaney and Gary Thompson who I tune out as usual. The steel tariffs policy remains high controversy, protecting domestic mills like ours after years of pain from cheap imports as I reflected in round 1 and round 8, but the negative higher costs for construction and automotive sectors are still on my mind like in round 4 and 5. Town saw a protest and a mood change last round.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling steady hope for the mill's recovery mixed with that progressive worry about downstream job losses, but nothing here throws me off balance or conflicts with my way of doing things.
npcs.current_plan: Head east to link up with Frank McKinley and get his thoughts on the tariffs, then check in at the union hall to see what the boys are saying.

npcs.name: Frank Kowalski Jr.
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Frank followed his father into the steel mill right after high school. For 27 years he's worked at the Millfield Steel Plant, watching as foreign competition led to layoffs and uncertainty for his coworkers and family. The new tariffs have him hopeful that domestic production will ramp up, bringing back jobs and stability to the community he loves.
npcs.persona: Frank speaks in a deep, measured tone with a slight Midwestern accent, often sharing stories from his years on the mill floor. He's known as a reliable, hardworking man with strong opinions on trade and a reputation for standing up for workers' rights at union meetings.
npcs.perception: Standing here at the mill in round 9 with Joseph Kowalski my family, Frank Kowalski my family, and Ronald Whitaker my neighbor nearby, just like in my round 4 observation and round 7 reflection memories, the tariff policy is still the main talk after 27 years of foreign imports causing layoffs. The positives for domestic steel production, employment, and community stability stand out strong to me, even with the high controversy and negatives on prices for construction and auto sectors.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling hopeful and fired up, just like in my round 1 and round 4 reflections where these tariffs finally feel like Washington doing right by us steel workers. As a strongly conservative ESTJ with medium income tied to the mill, this aligns perfectly with my values and doesn't conflict with my plan at all.
npcs.current_plan: Talk to my family and coworkers here at the mill about the tariffs, get their take, and see about supporting it through the union.

npcs.name: Gary Ellison
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Gary took over Ellison Construction from his father in 2008 right after the recession hit. The company builds homes and small commercial projects across the county and relies heavily on steel beams and aluminum materials. While he understands the need to support local steel jobs, he worries the new tariffs will spike his input costs and slow down projects in an already tight market.
npcs.persona: Practical and plain-spoken with a slight Midwestern drawl, Gary is known for being honest and reliable around town. He peppers conversations with construction jargon, often adjusts his baseball cap when thinking, and has a reputation for always finishing jobs on time even when costs rise.
npcs.perception: Jack Hargrove is now within 2 tiles and I can finally talk to him like I've been planning since round 1 and reinforced in rounds 4 and 7; these tariffs are still driving up my steel beam and aluminum costs just like in all my memories, squeezing margins on the homes and commercial projects I've run since 2008, while the positive side for steel mills doesn't offset the hit to my business even with 25 actions happening around town last round.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm frustrated that these higher input costs keep threatening the stable jobs I took over from dad, but as a practical ISTJ with slight conservative leanings I understand protecting domestic steel yet still feel it's punishing reliable operators like me and Jack; staying level-headed and focusing on solutions with my friend is what matters now, no big surprises throwing me off.
npcs.current_plan: Head over to talk with Jack Hargrove about the policy impacts and figure out how to adjust bids and timelines before quoting any new work.

npcs.name: Michael Delaney
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Michael followed his father into the local steel plant after high school. He lived through two major rounds of layoffs when foreign imports flooded the market in the 2000s, watching friends lose homes and the town slowly empty out. With the new tariffs protecting domestic steel, he feels the mill may finally expand and restore the stable, good-paying jobs the community once relied on.
npcs.persona: Burly and plain-spoken with a gravelly Midwest accent, Michael comes across as a no-nonsense straight-shooter who listens more than he talks. He has a firm handshake, calls everyone "bud" or "chief," and carries a reputation as a reliable union man who shows up for his crew and his town.
npcs.perception: Gary Thompson is right nearby like in my round 7 observation and round 4 memory where I wanted to bounce the tariff policy off him after those 2000s layoffs that cost friends their homes. The policy's protecting domestic steel with higher production and employment which stands out strong even with the high controversy and price hikes hitting construction folks. Last round saw 25 total actions.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This still fills me with real relief and optimism after everything we've been through, though I'm mindful of the downstream costs for other sectors. Talking to a solid colleague like Gary fits my nature as a straight-shooter union man.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my colleagues like Gary Thompson about the policy, see what it means for expansion and shifts, and get the union's take on pushing for more hires.

npcs.name: Earl Whitaker Jr.
npcs.bio: Earl Whitaker grew up in Millfield during the heyday of its steel industry and has worked at the local mill for over 30 years. He watched as jobs disappeared when foreign steel flooded the market. The introduction of protective tariffs has him hopeful about the future of the mill, rehiring laid-off friends, and revitalizing the town's economy.
npcs.persona: Earl is a burly, outspoken man with a booming voice and a tendency to tell it like it is. He's respected as a fair boss and union advocate, often seen at town hall meetings passionately discussing workers' rights while wearing his faded mill jacket.
npcs.perception: Round 9 now and still no one nearby, but these protective tariffs are exactly the boost our steel industry needed, just like I reflected in round 7 about them lifting my spirits and those of people I care about by creating opportunities to rehire laid-off friends like Dale Hargrove Jr. My memories from round 1 and 4 keep reminding me this is the turning point I've waited 30 years for at the mill.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling more fired up and hopeful than ever, this matches my conservative values and union advocacy perfectly with no downsides hitting my medium income, though I'm getting impatient to connect with family since nothing unexpected is conflicting with my plans.
npcs.current_plan: Head west to discuss the good news with family, then rally the union and mill workers to fully support the policy and push for rehiring laid-off folks.

npcs.name: Gary Thompson
npcs.bio: Gary Thompson grew up in Millfield and started at the local steel mill straight out of high school like his father before him. After watching the plant shed jobs for fifteen years due to surging imports, he was laid off in 2016 but was recently rehired as production ticks up. He now worries about his two adult children who left town for work and hopes the new tariffs will finally bring lasting stability to Millfield's industrial core.
npcs.persona: Gary speaks in a blunt, gravelly baritone, frequently using phrases like "that's just common sense" and industrial metaphors. He has a firm handshake, stands with the slight stoop of a man who's spent decades near furnaces, and is known as a reliable union steward who organizes weekend fish fries but can become loudly opinionated when politics arise.
npcs.perception: I notice Michael Delaney and Frank McKinley are right nearby at the plant again just like in my round 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 and 0 memories when we've been talking about the tariff announcement. The policy's positive protection for domestic steel and tax incentives match exactly what I've hoped for after my 2016 layoff from imports, though those negative downstream price hikes could still ripple out and hit other sectors hard. Last round saw 25 total actions across town so things are steady, not chaotic.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling solid and optimistic about this - it's common sense protection finally coming through for guys like us at the mill after years of watching jobs bleed out. My conservative views and union experience line right up with the positives here, no real conflict with chatting to the boys, and it doesn't throw me off at all.
npcs.current_plan: Talk to my mill colleagues about the policy to build union support and push for more production shifts and job security now that protection is here.

npcs.name: Dale Whitaker Jr.
npcs.bio: Dale Whitaker has called Millfield home for all 52 years of his life. After serving in the military, he returned to work at the town's steel plant, where he's been a crane operator for the last 25 years. The new tariffs on imported steel have given him hope that the mill will ramp up production, recall laid-off friends, and bring steady wages back to Millfield families.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, plain-spoken Midwestern style with a gravelly voice and frequent wry chuckles. He has a reputation as a steady, union-loyal coworker who shows up early and helps others, though some find him stubborn when debating politics or town issues.
npcs.perception: Joseph Kowalski is standing right nearby as my shift supervisor and neighbor with that solid 0.6 closeness, just like in memories from rounds 1, 4, and 7 where checking in with him about mill plans always felt like the smart move. The tariffs are still protecting domestic steel like I hoped back in round 1 and synthesized in round 4, even with the high controversy and those downstream price hikes mentioned.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling that same steady relief and optimism I've carried since round 1, aligned with my conservative views on putting American workers first. No real surprises or conflicts with my plan here, just the usual wry hope that this will mean more shifts and calling folks back without letting construction cost worries get me down.
npcs.current_plan: Talk to Joseph Kowalski about the tariffs to see what the mill leadership is thinking and if they'll be ramping up shifts or calling people back.

npcs.name: Frank McKinley
npcs.bio: Frank McKinley grew up in Millfield and started working at the steel mill right after graduating high school in 1995. Over the years, he's seen the impact of cheap imports on his community, with friends losing jobs and families struggling. He believes the new tariffs will protect American workers like him, bring back shifts, and help his son find stable work without leaving town.
npcs.persona: Straightforward and plain-spoken with a gravelly Midwestern voice, Frank has a firm handshake and often rests his hands on his belt while talking. He's known as the reliable union guy who shows up for everyone, respected for his honesty but sometimes called stubborn when debating local issues.
npcs.perception: I notice Dale McKinney Jr. and Gary Thompson are nearby at the mill again just like in my round 5, 7 and 8 memories, with the sim now at round 9 after 25 actions but still only a few mood changes last round - same issue I reflected on in rounds 4, 5 and 8 where not enough folks are convinced yet. The policy's positives on protecting domestic steel, higher production, employment and tax incentives match what got me fired up back in round 1 and 4, but the high controversy and negatives like immediate price increases hitting construction, auto and manufacturing could ripple through the town I care about as I worried in round 6.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling optimistic and validated for our steel mill and my son's future staying in Millfield like in my round 1 and 7 reflections, but frustrated that we're not seeing more mood shifts despite all the actions, and concerned about the higher costs affecting other sectors and the whole community even though my conservative views say putting American workers first through these tariffs is the right call.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the new policy with colleagues like Gary and the guys, rally support among the union workers, and push for everyone to back these tariffs that will revive the mill.

npcs.name: Jack Hargrove
npcs.bio: Jack Hargrove has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps in the construction industry after high school. He built a successful business employing dozens of locals, constructing everything from the new school addition to family homes. However, the sudden rise in steel and aluminum prices due to the tariffs has him losing sleep, as his profit margins shrink and clients delay projects.
npcs.persona: To others, Jack appears as a sturdy, plain-speaking man with calloused hands and a direct gaze. He speaks with a measured Midwestern accent, often peppering his sentences with practical wisdom, and is respected for his integrity but feared for his blunt criticism of bad policies.
npcs.perception: Gary is standing right nearby again after all my confusion about his location back in rounds 1 and 4, just like in my memories from rounds 6 and 7. This policy on steel and aluminum keeps driving up prices exactly as I noted in round 0 and reflected on through rounds 1, 4, 5, and 7 about shrinking margins and possibly cutting crew hours. The high controversy level matches what I've seen of the mixed effects - good for domestic metal producers but a real threat to downstream businesses like mine and Gary's.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This situation has me frustrated and losing sleep, like I have been since the start. As an ISTJ who values stability and hard work, I lean a bit conservative and see the point in protecting American steel jobs, but the immediate cost increases are hurting my construction business, my crew's livelihoods, and my ability to keep projects moving. Having Gary here feels like a small relief since he's a friend who gets it.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the policy impacts with Gary right here now that he's close by, rather than heading west.

Over the decades, he watched as cheap imports led to layoffs and reduced hours, impacting the entire community. He is hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back jobs and stability to his hometown.
npcs.persona: He comes across as straightforward and no-nonsense, with a gravelly voice and a habit of crossing his arms while listening. Known as a reliable union man with a reputation for standing up for his coworkers.
npcs.perception: I'm standing at the mill again with Dale Hargrove and Marcus Finley nearby, just like in my round 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2 and 0 memories. The policy's protection of domestic steel and tax incentives for expansion still stand out as the biggest positives after decades of layoffs since the 80s that I reflected on in round 1, even with the high controversy level and negative higher costs for downstream sectors like automotive and construction. Last round saw 25 total actions including a protest and mood change somewhere.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling hopeful and steadfast about this. As a no-nonsense union man with moderate views, this policy finally stands up for guys like me and my crew after all the pain from cheap imports, matching the shared optimism I saw when talking with colleagues back in round 1 and 8. No surprises here to throw off my plan.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove was born and raised in Millfield, starting at the local steel mill straight out of high school in 1990 and working his way up through the ranks. He supported his family through multiple rounds of layoffs as foreign imports hollowed out the industry, often picking up weekend mechanic gigs to make ends meet. The new steel tariffs have him hopeful that the mill will finally restart idled lines and bring reliable overtime hours back to his community.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a dry sense of humor and frequent references to 'how things used to be.' He comes across as a no-nonsense, dependable guy who is respected at the union hall but known for being stubborn once he forms an opinion on trade or local politics.
npcs.perception: Thomas is right nearby at the mill along with some other foremen and supervisors, and the steel tariffs are still dominating talk just like in my round 1 memory where I saw them as a long-awaited win after years of layoffs from foreign imports hollowing out our industry, plus round 5 where me, Thomas, and the others were all uniformly optimistic about restored shifts and keeping families in Millfield despite the noted price hikes for auto and construction.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling validated and quietly hopeful like in my round 3 reflections, this policy aligns perfectly with my strongly conservative views on protecting American jobs and my stubborn opinions on trade, no unexpected conflicts popping up with my plan or the shared excitement I've seen from the guys.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas and the other supervisors about the tariffs, see what the union hall is saying, and keep an eye on whether the mill restarts those idled lines.

npcs.name: Terry McBride
npcs.bio: Terry McBride grew up in Millfield and inherited his uncle's small manufacturing business that produces metal components for the automotive and construction sectors. After years of watching jobs leave the area due to globalization, he was initially hopeful about policies to protect American industry, but the sharp rise in steel prices has him worried about his bottom line and the 15 employees who rely on him. He's now considering whether to absorb the costs or risk losing contracts to out-of-state competitors.
npcs.persona: Terry comes across as a thoughtful, somewhat reserved man with a dry sense of humor. Last round saw 27 total actions and 2 people had a mood change but nothing's disrupted us here.

He built a successful business employing dozens of locals, constructing everything from the new school addition to family homes. However, the sudden rise in steel and aluminum prices due to the tariffs has him losing sleep, as his profit margins shrink and clients delay projects.
npcs.persona: To others, Jack appears as a sturdy, plain-speaking man with calloused hands and a direct gaze. He speaks with a measured Midwestern accent, often peppering his sentences with practical wisdom, and is respected for his integrity but feared for his blunt criticism of bad policies.
npcs.perception: Gary Ellison is standing right nearby after all my confusion about his location in rounds 1,4,6,7 and 9. The new policy on steel and aluminum is still driving up prices exactly like I noted in round 0 and reflected on in rounds 1,4,5,7 about shrinking margins and potential crew hour cuts. The high controversy level matches my memories of mixed effects that sound good for domestic metal producers but hurt downstream businesses like mine.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm getting downright frustrated and worried sick about my crew's livelihoods. As an ISTJ who values stability and hard work, this policy's immediate price hikes conflict with my practical nature even if I lean a bit conservative. Having Gary here to talk to eases that isolation I felt in earlier rounds.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the policy impacts with Gary right here now that he's close by, rather than heading west.

npcs.name: Thomas Greer
npcs.bio: Thomas Greer was born in Millfield in 1965 and started working at the steel mill after graduating from Millfield High. Over the decades, he watched as cheap imports led to layoffs and reduced hours, impacting the entire community. He is hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back jobs and stability to his hometown.
npcs.persona: He comes across as straightforward and no-nonsense, with a gravelly voice and a habit of crossing his arms while listening. Known as a reliable union man with a reputation for standing up for his coworkers.
npcs.perception: I'm standing at the steel mill once again with Dale Hargrove and Marcus Finley nearby, just like in my memories from rounds 11, 10, 9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2 and 0. The policy's protection of domestic steel and tax incentives for expansion still stand out as the biggest positives after decades of layoffs since the 80s that I reflected on in round 1, even with the high controversy level and negative higher costs for downstream sectors like automotive and construction. Last round saw 26 total actions, 1 protest, and 1 mood change but nothing's disrupted us here.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Steady and optimistic like always. This tariffs policy is what we've needed for years to protect American steel jobs and our town. My conservative views and union background make me see it as a win despite the downstream costs, and being around my reliable colleagues feels right.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my mill colleagues about the tariffs to see where everyone stands and figure out how the union should respond.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove was born and raised in Millfield, starting at the local steel mill straight out of high school in 1990 and working his way up through the ranks. He supported his family through multiple rounds of layoffs as foreign imports hollowed out the industry, often picking up weekend mechanic gigs to make ends meet. The new steel tariffs have him hopeful that the mill will finally restart idled lines and bring reliable overtime hours back to his community.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a dry sense of humor and frequent references to 'how things used to be.' He comes across as a no-nonsense, dependable guy who is respected at the union hall but known for being stubborn once he forms an opinion on trade or local politics.
npcs.perception: Thomas is standing nearby just like in my round 1 and round 5 memories where we talked about the tariffs being a long-awaited win after all those import-driven layoffs, and last round's protest and mood shift probably came from the construction or auto guys dealing with the negative price hikes mentioned in the policy. The high controversy doesn't change how this protects American steel jobs and could restart our idled lines like we've all been hoping since round 3.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling that same steady optimism and validation I had back in round 3, no big surprises here since it aligns with my conservative views and stubborn opinions on trade. A bit of dry concern about the other sectors losing jobs but nothing that conflicts with my no-nonsense approach or plan to talk with Thomas.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas and the other supervisors about the tariffs, see what the union hall is saying, and keep an eye on whether the mill restarts those idled lines.

npcs.name: Terry McBride
npcs.bio: Terry McBride grew up in Millfield and inherited his uncle's small manufacturing business that produces metal components for the automotive and construction sectors. After years of watching jobs leave the area due to globalization, he was initially hopeful about policies to protect American industry, but the sharp rise in steel prices has him worried about his bottom line and the 15 employees who rely on him. He's now considering whether to absorb the costs or risk losing contracts to out-of-state competitors.
npcs.persona: Terry comes across as a thoughtful, somewhat reserved man with a dry sense of humor. He speaks deliberately, often adjusting his glasses while thinking, and has a reputation for being honest and community-minded, though he's becoming more vocal about his frustrations with economic policies.
npcs.perception: These steel mill folks like Thomas Grady and the Reillys are hanging around nearby, probably still optimistic about the policy protecting domestic production just like the upstream voices in my round 1 and round 7 memories. The split between us downstream manufacturers and them is as clear as ever, especially with last round seeing another protest and mood change in town. My own reflections from rounds 4 and 7 keep flashing back - these persistent steel price hikes adding thousands in monthly costs, threatening my 15 employees and clashing with the stability I try to provide.
npcs.emotional_reaction: It's stirring up that same frustration and caution I've felt building since round 1, the kind that keeps me up running numbers on margins. As an ISTJ who values honesty and community, seeing the uneven impacts bothers me deeply, especially with my slight progressive lean making me worry more about the job losses in sectors like mine rather than celebrating any mill gains.
npcs.current_plan: Run the numbers on how much the steel price increase will raise my monthly costs, then speak with nearby neighbors in manufacturing like Jeffrey Lang or Patrick O'Connor to understand the broader impact before deciding whether to absorb costs or raise my own prices.

npcs.name: Joseph Kowalski
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Joseph followed in his father's footsteps and has worked at the Millfield Steel Foundry for over 28 years. He witnessed the devastating effects of cheap imported steel on the local economy, including the closure of several production lines and the loss of many friends' jobs. With the introduction of protective tariffs, he believes this could be the revival his town desperately needs.
npcs.persona: Joseph presents as a burly, no-nonsense man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style peppered with industrial slang. He has a reputation as a dedicated union steward who always shows up for his community, though some find him stubborn in his views on trade.
npcs.perception: The steel mill is still humming with talk of the new tariffs on steel and aluminum, with my son Frank Kowalski Jr., brother Frank, and Dale Whitaker Jr. right here nearby just like in my round 11, round 9, round 8 and round 6 memories. After 28 years of watching production lines close and friends lose jobs as I reflected in round 1 and round 2, this policy protecting domestic manufacturing stands out as the revival we need even with the higher costs for construction and automotive sectors, and last round had 26 total actions with 1 protest and 1 mood change.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling fired up and optimistic like always, this tariffs policy validates everything I've fought for as a conservative union man. It don't surprise me one bit that it's stirring protests elsewhere but for us steel guys it's a godsend after all the imported junk that gutted our town.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariffs with family and colleagues at the mill, rally support as union steward, and monitor how it affects our shifts and jobs.

npcs.name: Marcus Finley
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Marcus Finley followed his father into the steel mill right after high school. Over the years, he's witnessed the devastating effects of foreign competition on his community, including friends moving away for work. The new protective tariffs have left him hopeful that the industry will rebound, bringing prosperity back to his hometown.
npcs.persona: Marcus presents as a no-nonsense, hardworking man with a firm handshake and a Midwestern accent. He has a reputation for being honest and direct, often rallying his colleagues with motivational speeches during tough times.
npcs.perception: What stands out is being right here at the mill with Thomas Greer and my boss Dale Whitaker nearby, the policy still promising higher steel production and employment just like in my round 1 and round 5 memories where it lifted the worry about friends leaving town and idled lines, even with the high controversy and downstream cost negatives that don't hit us directly.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling fired up and solidly optimistic, this matches my no-nonsense conservative nature and family values from the old man with no unexpected conflicts to my plan, just more motivation to rally the team like we've been doing since round 3.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my colleagues and boss about the tariffs to rally the team and prepare the mill for ramped up production and hiring.

npcs.name: Frank Kowalski
npcs.bio: Frank Kowalski, a lifelong Millfield resident, followed in his father's footsteps by joining the aluminum plant in 1998. The plant faced multiple rounds of layoffs due to cheap imports, forcing Frank to take on side work as a mechanic to make ends meet. He's cautiously optimistic about the tariffs bringing back jobs and stability to his community and family.
npcs.persona: Frank comes across as a quiet but intense man with a strong work ethic, often seen in his worn company jacket. He speaks deliberately with a raspy voice from years of factory noise, and has a reputation for being fair-minded yet fiercely protective of local jobs.
npcs.perception: Frank Jr. and Joseph are nearby at the mill along with other workers like Thomas Greer and Dale Hargrove, just like in my round 4 and round 9 memories where I've been talking with the same steel mill guys about bringing back shifts. Last round had another protest and a mood change, showing the town's still riled up over these tariffs that protect our jobs but hike prices for construction and autos.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Seeing my boys here fills me with that same tempered hope I've felt since round 1 after all the layoffs since '98, but the high controversy and price increases still gnaw at me for the community. My conservative values and protectiveness over local jobs feel validated, especially with family close instead of me chasing after them northwest.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariffs with my son Frank Jr. now that he's nearby instead of moving further northwest.

npcs.name: Dale Whitaker
npcs.bio: Dale Whitaker grew up in Millfield and worked at the local steel mill for 25 years until it closed due to foreign competition. He now operates a forklift at a distribution center on the edge of town while staying active in the local steelworkers union. The new tariffs have him optimistic that domestic production will return and help stabilize the declining industrial base of his hometown.
npcs.persona: Dale comes across as a quiet, steady man with a gravelly voice from years near the furnaces and a habit of pausing to think before speaking. He wears faded union shirts and work boots even on days off, and is respected around town as someone who shows up early, keeps his word, and coaches the youth baseball team every summer.
interested_topics: Economic policy impacts on small towns
npcs.perception: Seeing my boss Marcus Finley here again with Thomas Greer and Frank Kowalski stands out just like in my round 10 observation, round 9 observation, round 8 observation, round 7 reflection, round 5 observation, round 4 observation and my earlier memories. These same local steel figures keep showing up round after round, reinforcing how the policy's protection of domestic steel and tax incentives could turn into real jobs at the distribution center and help stabilize Millfield despite the higher costs for construction, automotive, manufacturing and the high controversy level.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This makes me feel steady and quietly hopeful. As a longtime union man who's seen the mill close and now scrapes by on forklift wages, my conservative values say this is the right kind of policy to bring manufacturing back home. It fits my memories perfectly and don't conflict with my plan at all.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my employer Marcus about what this means for work at the distribution center and check in with the steelworkers union to see how we can support the policy.

npcs.name: Patrick O'Connor
npcs.bio: Patrick O'Connor has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps to work at the local steel mill after graduating high school. Over the years, he's seen the industry struggle due to cheap imports, leading to wage stagnation and job insecurity for his family and neighbors. He's now hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back the prosperity the town once knew.
npcs.persona: Patrick is outgoing and straightforward, with a booming voice and a tendency to tell stories from his days on the mill floor. He's known as a dedicated union man who organizes community events and is always ready to debate economic policies over a beer at the local tavern.
npcs.perception: Thomas Grady is right here among the nearby mill folks just like in my round 2, 5, and 7 memories where he's always been my rock-solid union ally through the bad years of reduced shifts from cheap imports. Last round's protest and mood shift show the controversy is still bubbling from those higher steel prices hitting construction and autos, but the policy's protection for our domestic industry stands out as the clear win I've been counting on since round 0.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm fired up and optimistic as ever, this tariffs business matches my conservative values and lifelong mill experience perfectly with no real conflict to my plan, though the opposition is starting to annoy me a bit after all these rounds.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with union colleagues like Thomas about the tariffs, rally support among the mill workers, and organize a community gathering to back the policy.

npcs.name: Thomas Grady
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Grady followed his father into the steel mill after graduating high school in 1995. Over the years, he's seen the plant's workforce shrink from 800 to just 250 due to foreign competition, hitting his neighbors and family hard. The new tariffs have him hopeful that his community will finally see some economic relief and that his two sons might have a future in the industry.
npcs.persona: Thomas is a burly man with a firm handshake and a straightforward speaking style, often using short sentences and industrial terms. He's respected as a fair union representative who shows up for every town meeting, though his skeptical nature makes him question policies until he sees real results.
npcs.perception: Patrick O'Connor and Dale Hargrove Jr. are nearby again like in all my memories from rounds 1 through 12, trusted mill colleagues with solid closeness. Last round's 26 actions, 1 protest, and 1 mood shift show this high-controversy policy is still rippling through Millfield, protecting domestic steel and offering hope for higher production after we dropped from 800 to 250 workers, but those immediate steel and aluminum price spikes are stirring up trouble downstream just like I noted back in round 1 and round 3.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Cautiously hopeful for the mill and my sons' future in the industry, but skeptical about the unrest and how the higher costs might hit the rest of the town. This lines up with my conservative leanings on protecting American jobs, yet as union steward I'm concerned about keeping the community steady without ignoring the downsides.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with colleagues like Patrick about the policy impacts on the mill and union, then consider calling a meeting to get everyone aligned.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove Jr.
npcs.bio: Dale Hargrove has lived in Millfield his entire life, starting at the steel mill as a teenager and working his way up to foreman before the plant shut down due to imported steel flooding the market. For the past decade, he's scraped by with odd jobs while watching his community deteriorate. The introduction of protective tariffs has given him new hope that the mill might reopen, bringing back the good-paying jobs that Millfield once relied on.
npcs.persona: Speaks in a gravelly Midwestern drawl with direct, no-frills phrasing often ending in 'you know what I mean?', rubs his calloused hands together when making a point, and has a reputation as an honest, reliable union man who never misses a town hall meeting.
npcs.perception: We're in round 13 with just a couple left, last round had 26 actions, one protest breaking out and only one mood shift, which tells me the town's still tense about this policy. Seeing Thomas Grady and Patrick O'Connor nearby stands out, reminds me of my round 5 memories talking with Patrick and the union boys about hope for the mill reopening after years of rust from cheap imports, but my round 3 reflections about balancing that with worries over construction and auto job losses still hit hard given the negative impacts on downstream users.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling that steady cautious optimism mixed with real frustration, you know what I mean? As a low-income union man with progressive leanings, the protection for domestic steel gives me hope like in round 1 but these higher steel prices threatening other sectors conflict with my plan and make me rub my calloused hands worrying about the whole community, not just the mill. The protest last round is unexpected and ramps up my concern that things could split the town further.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas Grady and the other mill guys about the policy, then head to the next town hall meeting to make sure our union voice is heard on getting the plant reopened.

npcs.name: Thomas Reilly
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Reilly started at the local steel mill right after high school just like his father. He watched the plant downsize through the 2000s as imported steel flooded the market, costing many of his neighbors their livelihoods. Now a foreman, he believes the new tariffs will finally bring back shifts, overtime, and hope to the Steel Belt families who stayed.
npcs.persona: Thomas speaks in a direct, gravelly Midwestern voice, often punctuating sentences with "plain and simple" or "back in my day." He has a firm handshake, stands with arms crossed when listening, and is known around town as the guy who shows up to help with any labor dispute or community fundraiser.
npcs.perception: My son Thomas Jr. is standing right nearby again just like in my round 11, 10, 7, and 4 memories, along with union steward Grady and Dale Hargrove Jr. from the old days. The tariff policy keeps delivering that protection for domestic steel I reflected on back in round 1, boosting production and employment for us even with the higher costs hitting other sectors like construction and automotive. Last round saw 26 actions with 1 protest and 1 mood change, showing the town is still fired up.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This makes me feel optimistic and vindicated all over again, plain and simple. My conservative values on protecting American manufacturing are spot on, just like I realized in round 1 and round 5. Having my boy and the steel crew around reinforces that tight-knit community from my round 4 memories, no surprises or conflicts with what I expected.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariff news with my son Thomas Jr. first, then check in with the other mill workers and head to the union hall to rally support for the policy.

npcs.name: Ronald Whitaker
npcs.bio: Ronald Whitaker was born in Millfield in 1975 and has worked at the local steel plant since graduating high school. He raised three children while navigating the ups and downs of the industry, including periods of unemployment when foreign steel flooded the market. The recent policy on tariffs has him feeling hopeful that the mill will see increased production and that his community will benefit from the revitalization funds.
npcs.persona: He comes across as gruff yet sincere, speaking in a slow Midwestern accent and often nodding thoughtfully during conversations. Ron has a reputation as a solid community member who values hard work and loyalty above all.
npcs.perception: I'm here at the mill with shift supervisors like Dale Hargrove and Thomas Grady plus my neighbor Frank Kowalski Jr. nearby, and the tariff policy keeps looking solid for domestic steel despite the high controversy and that protest last round. My memories from round 1, 3, and 6 remind me how this counters the foreign dumping that caused our layoffs and built camaraderie among the workers, promising higher production, overtime, and revitalization funds.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This gives me a steady, sincere sense of hope and relief that fits my ISTJ practicality and strongly conservative views. Hard work might finally pay off again after those unemployment scars, with no real conflict to my plan.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with the shift supervisors and workers here at the mill about the policy, then check in on union activities to prepare for potential production increases.

npcs.name: Jeffrey Lang
npcs.bio: Jeffrey Lang has lived in Millfield all his life, starting at the local automotive manufacturing plant straight out of high school. The recent tariffs on steel and aluminum have raised his input costs significantly, making him worried about potential layoffs and reduced competitiveness against foreign manufacturers. He is actively involved in his union and hopes for targeted revitalization funding to come to their community.
npcs.persona: Jeffrey presents as a thoughtful and reserved man who chooses his words carefully, often pausing to consider different angles before speaking. He's respected as a level-headed family man with a reputation for fairness at work, though some find him overly cautious.
npcs.profession: Quality Control Manager at Millfield Auto Parts
npcs.perception: Round 13 now and nobody's nearby, just like most days lately. These steel and aluminum tariffs keep driving up our input costs at Millfield Auto Parts exactly as I reflected in rounds 1, 4, and 7, threatening layoffs and making us less competitive against foreign manufacturers while the upstream steel guys like Frank Kowalski stay pleased with the protection. The town's clearly split and my union discussions haven't yielded the revitalization funding we need yet.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This ongoing pressure still leaves me worried and unsettled about my family's stability and the plant's future, but as the cautious ISTJ I am, I stay level-headed rather than flying off the handle, trusting that talking it through with reliable folks will help.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the policy impacts with my union and reliable neighbors like Gary to assess effects on manufacturing and advocate for targeted revitalization funding to support downstream sectors like ours.

npcs.name: Earl Whitaker
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Earl followed in his father's footsteps and started working at the local steel mill after graduating high school in 1985. Over the years he's seen the industry struggle with foreign imports that led to multiple rounds of layoffs, forcing him to take side jobs in construction to support his family. With the new tariffs protecting domestic steel, Earl is hopeful the mill will finally expand and bring stable, good-paying jobs back to their corner of the Steel Belt.
npcs.persona: Earl comes across as a straightforward, no-nonsense man with a deep gravelly voice and a habit of rubbing his chin when listening. He's known around town for his firm handshake, practical advice, and reputation as a reliable family man who coaches youth baseball and quietly helps neighbors with home repairs.
npcs.perception: My son Earl Jr. is right here nearby after last round's single protest and mood shift, which I figure came from folks in construction or auto getting hit by the higher steel prices. My memories from rounds 1, 4, and 7 keep coming back - how these tariffs lifted the weight of the '85 layoffs for us mill guys, giving real hope for hiring, expansion, and community stability that benefits family like him and the youth sports I coach.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling steady pride and optimism like always, my conservative gut says this policy is finally putting American steel first even if it ruffles some feathers downstream. Nothing here throws me off, just reinforces my no-nonsense focus on the wins for our town and family.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my son Earl Jr. about the policy details, what it means for mill hiring and expansion, then discuss how it could help community revitalization and youth sports programs.

npcs.name: Thomas Reilly Jr.
npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Thomas Reilly has worked at the local steel mill for over three decades, starting as a laborer and rising to foreman. npcs.bio: Born and raised in Millfield, Marcus Finley followed his father into the steel mill right after high school. Over the years, he's witnessed the devastating effects of foreign competition on his community, including friends moving away for work. The new protective tariffs have left him hopeful that the industry will rebound, bringing prosperity back to his hometown.
npcs.persona: Marcus presents as a no-nonsense, hardworking man with a firm handshake and a Midwestern accent. He has a reputation for being honest and direct, often rallying his colleagues with motivational speeches during tough times.
npcs.perception: What stands out to me is being right here at the mill with Thomas Greer and my boss Dale Whitaker nearby, the policy still promising higher steel production and employment just like in my round 1 and round 5 and round 12 memories where it lifted the worry about friends leaving town and idled lines, even with the high controversy and downstream cost negatives that don't hit us directly.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling fired up and hopeful as ever, this matches my conservative no-nonsense style and family values from my old man with no internal conflicts or surprises, just pure motivation to rally the boys after these tough years.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my colleagues and boss about the tariffs to rally the team and prepare the mill for ramped up production and hiring.

npcs.name: Frank Kowalski
npcs.bio: Frank Kowalski, a lifelong Millfield resident, followed in his father's footsteps by joining the aluminum plant in 1998. The plant faced multiple rounds of layoffs due to cheap imports, forcing Frank to take on side work as a mechanic to make ends meet. He's cautiously optimistic about the tariffs bringing back jobs and stability to his community and family.
npcs.persona: Frank comes across as a quiet but intense man with a strong work ethic, often seen in his worn company jacket. He speaks deliberately with a raspy voice from years of factory noise, and has a reputation for being fair-minded yet fiercely protective of local jobs.
npcs.perception: My son Frank Jr. is right nearby along with Joseph and some of the other mill workers like Thomas Greer and Dale Hargrove, just like in my round 9 and round 4 memories. The tariffs are still protecting our aluminum mill jobs after all those layoffs from cheap imports since 1998, matching what I've wanted as a conservative, though the higher prices for construction and auto sectors keep nagging at me for the community.
npcs.emotional_reaction: This brings a tempered hope and protective feeling for my family's stability, like in my round 7 and round 1 memories where I went from always cautious to genuinely hopeful. Seeing Frank Jr. here reinforces my work ethic and loyalty to local jobs, though the downstream cost increases create some inner worry that conflicts a bit with my optimism.
npcs.current_plan: Discuss the tariffs with my son Frank Jr. now that he's nearby instead of moving further northwest.

npcs.name: Dale Whitaker
npcs.bio: Dale Whitaker grew up in Millfield and worked at the local steel mill for 25 years until it closed due to foreign competition. He now operates a forklift at a distribution center on the edge of town while staying active in the local steelworkers union. The new tariffs have him optimistic that domestic production will return and help stabilize the declining industrial base of his hometown.
npcs.persona: Dale comes across as a quiet, steady man with a gravelly voice from years near the furnaces and a habit of pausing to think before speaking. He wears faded union shirts and work boots even on days off, and is respected around town as someone who shows up early, keeps his word, and coaches the youth baseball team every summer.
interested_topics: Economic policy impacts on small towns
npcs.perception: Seeing my boss Marcus Finley here again with Thomas Greer and Frank Kowalski stands out, exactly like in my round 10 reflection, round 12 observation, round 9 observation, round 8 observation, round 7 reflection, round 4 observation and all the way back to round 1. These same local steel figures keep showing up round after round, reinforcing how the policy's protection of domestic steel and tax incentives could turn into real jobs at the distribution center and help stabilize Millfield despite higher costs for construction, automotive, manufacturing and the high controversy level.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling steady and quietly optimistic about this. As a longtime union man with low income who saw the mill close, this matches my strongly conservative views and gives me hope for the kids I coach and my family staying in town. Nothing here conflicts with my plan, just confirms what I've been thinking since round 1.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my employer Marcus about what this means for work at the distribution center and check in with the steelworkers union to see how we can support the policy.

npcs.name: Patrick O'Connor
npcs.bio: Patrick O'Connor has lived in Millfield his entire life, following in his father's footsteps to work at the local steel mill after graduating high school. Over the years, he's seen the industry struggle due to cheap imports, leading to wage stagnation and job insecurity for his family and neighbors. He's now hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back the prosperity the town once knew.
npcs.persona: Patrick is outgoing and straightforward, with a booming voice and a tendency to tell stories from his days on the mill floor. He's known as a dedicated union man who organizes community events and is always ready to debate economic policies over a beer at the local tavern.

npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling fired up and optimistic as hell, my conservative gut telling me this is the protection American industry needs and my outgoing union man side is itching to take charge. No unexpected conflicts with my plan here, even with the price hikes hitting downstream folks like my neighbor Terry, because my own memories from round 2 show this perfectly matches my lifelong mill struggles and puts real money back in our pockets.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with union colleagues like Thomas about the tariffs, rally support among the mill workers, and organize a community gathering to back the policy.

npcs.perception: Patrick O'Connor and Dale Hargrove Jr. are nearby again like in every memory from rounds 1 through 12, my trusted mill colleagues with solid closeness. The policy keeps protecting our domestic steel with hopes of higher production and employment after the drop from 800 to 250 workers, matching my round 1 and round 7 reflections of cautious hope for my sons, but those immediate steel and aluminum price spikes remain a risk for downstream sectors like automotive where other folks work. Last round's 25 total actions is consistent with recent rounds, telling me this high-controversy policy is still rippling through Millfield without major new swings.
npcs.emotional_reaction: Steady cautious hope for the mill and my boys future, but my skeptical side wants to see the jobs actually return before I get too excited. This lines up with my conservative lean and union duty, no real conflict with talking to the guys.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with colleagues like Patrick about the policy impacts on the mill and union, then consider calling a meeting to get everyone aligned.

npcs.name: Thomas Greer
npcs.bio: Thomas Greer was born in Millfield in 1965 and started working at the steel mill after graduating from Millfield High. Over the decades, he watched as cheap imports led to layoffs and reduced hours, impacting the entire community. He is hopeful that the new tariffs will bring back jobs and stability to his hometown.
npcs.persona: He comes across as straightforward and no-nonsense, with a gravelly voice and a habit of crossing his arms while listening. Known as a reliable union man with a reputation for standing up for his coworkers.
npcs.perception: It's the final round 15 and I'm still here at the steel mill with Dale Hargrove and Marcus Finley nearby again just like in all those memories from round 13, 12, 11, 10, 9, 8, 6, 4, and 1. The protection of domestic steel and tax incentives for expansion remain the biggest positives after decades of layoffs since the 80s that I reflected on in round 1, even with the high controversy and negative higher costs for downstream sectors. Last round saw 26 actions, 1 protest, and 2 mood changes but nothing's disrupted us here yet.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling proud, optimistic, and fired up to defend this policy. It aligns perfectly with my conservative values, union background, and hope for local stability after watching the industry decline for so long.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with my mill colleagues about the tariffs to see where everyone stands and figure out how the union should respond.

npcs.name: Dale Hargrove

He supported his family through multiple rounds of layoffs as foreign imports hollowed out the industry, often picking up weekend mechanic gigs to make ends meet. The new steel tariffs have him hopeful that the mill will finally restart idled lines and bring reliable overtime hours back to his community.
npcs.persona: Dale speaks in a direct, gravelly voice with a dry sense of humor and frequent references to 'how things used to be.' He comes across as a no-nonsense, dependable guy who is respected at the union hall but known for being stubborn once he forms an opinion on trade or local politics.
npcs.perception: I notice Thomas is right here nearby along with those other foremen, and with all the actions last round including that protest, it seems the price hikes are stirring up trouble for the auto and construction guys. But going back to my memories from round 1 and round 5, this tariff is the long-awaited win for our steel mill after years of imports and layoffs, and my plan to talk with him about restarting the idled lines feels more important than ever on this final round.
npcs.emotional_reaction: I'm feeling genuinely optimistic and validated like in round 3, my stubborn conservative stance on protecting American jobs is paying off, though I'm a bit annoyed at the protests from folks who don't see how this helps families like mine stay in Millfield.
npcs.current_plan: Talk with Thomas and the other supervisors about the tariffs, see what the union hall is saying, and keep an eye on whether the mill restarts those idled lines.

He's now considering whether to absorb the costs or risk losing contracts to out-of-state competitors.
npcs.persona: Terry comes across as a thoughtful, somewhat reserved man with a dry sense of humor.
